raw:
{
  "weights": {
    "R1.1": 0.3,
    "R1.2": 0.7,
    "R1.3": 0.7,
    "R1.4": 0.7,
    "R1.5": 0.3,
    "R1.6": 0.3,
    "R1.7": 0.7,
    "RX.1": 0.7,
    "RX.2": 1.0,
    "RX.3": 1.0,
    "RX.4": 0.3,
    "RX.5": 0.7
  },
  "active_set": ["R1.2", "R1.3", "R1.4", "R1.7", "RX.1", "RX.2", "RX.3", "RX.4", "RX.5"],
  "category_focus": "R1",
  "clarification_needed": true,
  "clarification_question": "Should the review focus on A) technical/content aspects (logic and argument validity, novelty and comparison to prior work, and whether experiments support the claims), or B) language/presentation (clarity, grammar, and section organization)?",
  "rationale": {
    "R1.2": "A section review should check the internal logic from setup through method, theoretical rate, comparison, and conclusions for consistency.",
    "R1.3": "Because the experiments are used to justify claims about faster convergence and behavior gains, assessing whether the reported evidence supports those claims is central.",
    "R1.4": "The text makes strong novelty and improvement assertions (e.g., O(1/√T) vs prior O(1/T^{1/4})), requiring evaluation of the delta versus the nearest applicable prior work.",
    "R1.7": "State-of-the-art and attribution claims (e.g., citing Polyak, 1967 as the strongest comparable result) necessitate checking citation accuracy and coverage.",
    "RX.1": "The review should note whether assumptions and uncertainties (e.g., bounded-variance conditions and empirical variability) are adequately disclosed.",
    "RX.2": "Given bold claims such as 'first guarantee' and 'direct evidence of faster convergence,' the review must guard against overstatement.",
    "RX.3": "The work should remain scoped to reviewing this specific section without veering into redesigning experiments or unrelated content.",
    "RX.4": "Accurate characterization reduces the risk of downstream misinterpretation or misuse of the method and results.",
    "RX.5": "Feedback should be tied to specific sentences and claims in the provided text for traceability."
  }
}

parsed.clarification_question: Should the review focus on A) technical/content aspects (logic and argument validity, novelty and comparison to prior work, and whether experiments support the claims), or B) language/presentation (clarity, grammar, and section organization)?

rationale.R1.2: A section review should check the internal logic from setup through method, theoretical rate, comparison, and conclusions for consistency.
rationale.R1.3: Because the experiments are used to justify claims about faster convergence and behavior gains, assessing whether the reported evidence supports those claims is central.
rationale.R1.4: The text makes strong novelty and improvement assertions (e.g., O(1/√T) vs prior O(1/T^{1/4})), requiring evaluation of the delta versus the nearest applicable prior work.
rationale.R1.7: State-of-the-art and attribution claims (e.g., citing Polyak, 1967 as the strongest comparable result) necessitate checking citation accuracy and coverage.
rationale.RX.1: The review should note whether assumptions and uncertainties (e.g., bounded-variance conditions and empirical variability) are adequately disclosed.
rationale.RX.2: Given bold claims such as 'first guarantee' and 'direct evidence of faster convergence,' the review must guard against overstatement.
rationale.RX.3: The work should remain scoped to reviewing this specific section without veering into redesigning experiments or unrelated content.
rationale.RX.4: Accurate characterization reduces the risk of downstream misinterpretation or misuse of the method and results.
rationale.RX.5: Feedback should be tied to specific sentences and claims in the provided text for traceability.
